title: Case Studies
subtitle: Real problems at real companies. Using public data.

cards.title: CYCLE's fleet is already an ad network.
cards.summary: CYCLE operates 14,000 e-bikes across six countries, covering 200M km a year in the densest parts of major European cities — the same always-moving urban footprint that turned Uber and Lyft's ad businesses into billion-dollar revenue lines. Built as an unsolicited proposal for CYCLE's Product Manager role, this case study includes a full business case, a functional spec, and a working prototype.
cards.meta: July 2026 · Deck + spec + working prototype · ~15 min read

cards.title: Babbel can write lessons with AI in seconds. Trust is the bottleneck.
cards.summary: Babbel learners run out of lessons, and hand-writing one small pack takes about 35 hours. AI can draft them in seconds — but a wrong grammar rule or a joke that doesn't translate erodes trust, as Babbel's biggest competitor learned publicly. This case study proposes a quality gate that sorts every AI-written lesson into green, yellow, or red: safe drafts ship instantly, risky ones reach a human first. It comes with a business case and a working prototype.
cards.meta: July 2026 · Deck + working prototype · ~10 min read

cards.title: "GetYourGuide" is built around the activity. Travellers think in trips.
Duolingo - Betting on AI Without Breaking the Streak
Airbnb and the Art of Doing Things That Don't Scale

getYourGuide.title: From Activities to Trips
getYourGuide.subtitle: GetYourGuide is built around activities. Travellers think in trips.
getYourGuide.summary: GetYourGuide enters 2026 from a position of strength - €1B in revenue, the first profitable year in its history, and a market debut on the horizon. But the product is built around the activity as its basic unit, while the traveller's unit of intent is the trip. This case maps the architectural gap and what closing it would require.

edtech.title: Zistre - A Platform for Global Test Preparation.
edtech.subtitle: CCAT · Predictive Index · Wonderlic · Watson Glaser · SHL · GRE · TOEFL - outcome-driven assessment prep for global learners.

sections.heading: The problem
paragraphs: Existing test-prep platforms were optimized for content volume, not candidate outcomes. Students were drowning in practice questions but never learning HOW to think through problems. Most platforms treated every learner the same - regardless of their starting level, learning pace, or target assessment. The result: high churn, low completion rates, and candidates who could memorize answers but couldn't transfer skills to new question types.

sections.heading: The insight
paragraphs: After talking to 100+ candidates who had failed their target assessments, a clear pattern emerged: the problem wasn't knowledge - it was thinking. Candidates needed structured frameworks for approaching unfamiliar problems, not more content to memorize. This insight became the foundation of the entire product.

sections.heading: What I built
paragraphs: An outcome-driven assessment prep platform covering 6 major test categories: CCAT, Wonderlic, Watson Glaser, SHL, GRE, and TOEFL. Not a course library - a product designed around measurable candidate outcomes.

features.title: Outcome-focused curriculum
features.body: Courses structured around thinking frameworks, not content dumps. Each module taught a transferable problem-solving approach.

features.title: 1:1 mentoring built into the product
features.body: Every candidate received personalized guidance. I used their practice scores and completion data to adapt the approach in real-time.

features.title: Adaptive difficulty progression
features.body: Content difficulty adjusted based on candidate performance, ensuring they were always learning at their edge - not too easy, not overwhelming.

features.title: Multi-format assessment coverage
features.body: Six different test categories, each with unique question types and strategies - from logical reasoning (CCAT) to verbal comprehension (GRE/TOEFL).

sections.heading: What I owned
paragraphs: Everything. This was my product from day one.
list: Continuous iteration based on outcome data

impactStats.label: of candidates passed their target assessment

sections.heading: What I learned
paragraphs: The biggest lesson: outcomes beat features. Every product decision was measured against one question - does this help candidates pass? Not 'does this look good' or 'will this increase time-on-site.' Just: does it work? That discipline shaped everything from curriculum design to pricing.

The second lesson: personal mentoring doesn't scale - but the insights from it do. By personally guiding 15000+ candidates, I built an intuition for learning patterns that no analytics dashboard could provide. Those patterns then informed the product design, making the platform better for everyone, including candidates I never spoke to directly.
